title: For this task, you are asked to provide a short, written reflection that is based on your clinical experiences to date. …

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
For this task, you are asked to provide a short, written reflection that is based on your clinical experiences to date. Within the reflection, you will take time to retrospectively examine a topic that has challenged your thinking and understanding of professionalism during your time in MD2. This task provides you with an opportunity to demonstrate your growing capacity for self-regulated learning, by demonstrating thoughtful insight into your abilities and limitations in a clinical setting.

Word limit: 500 words

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
During my time in MD2, a topic that has significantly challenged my understanding of professionalism is navigating difficult conversations with patients and their families. These conversations include delivering poor prognoses, addressing non-compliance with medical treatment, and discussing end-of-life care. As a future physician, a deep understanding of professionalism entails not only displaying technical prowess but also employing emotional intelligence, active listening, and effective communication skills to provide optimal care for patients. Reflecting on my clinical experiences, two instances stand out that have prompted me to reconsider the way I approach difficult conversations with patients.

In the first scenario, I was shadowing my attending physician as he delivered the news of a terminal cancer diagnosis to a patient and her family. The patient had a long history of smoking, and this habit contributed to her lung cancer diagnosis. When delivering the news, my attending physician was candid and sensitive, effectively balancing the gravity of the situation with empathy. However, the patient's son, overwhelmed with emotions, reacted defensively and began blaming the physician for not doing enough for his mother. My attending physician maintained his composure and gave the family time to process the news, and then brilliantly redirected the conversation to focus on the patient and her treatment options. This showcased the importance of emotional intelligence, which will allow me to better connect with patients and their families and establish trust during the most trying of times.

The second scenario involved a patient with diabetes and multiple complications that resulted from poor compliance to their prescribed medication and lifestyle modifications. When speaking with the patient, I found it challenging not to appear judgmental while addressing their non-adherence to the treatment plan. I realized that my inability to establish rapport with the patient inhibited an open and honest conversation. Consequently, I approached my attending physician for advice, and he emphasized the significance of understanding the patient's perspective, addressing barriers to compliance, and providing tailored educational information and support. Applying these lessons in subsequent conversations with the patient resulted in improved rapport, and the patient subsequently displayed greater motivation in managing their diabetes. This experience taught me the importance of adaptability and flexibility while taking into consideration the unique circumstances of each patient.

These two experiences have been instrumental in shaping my understanding of professionalism, specifically effective communication, in the clinical setting. To further improve upon my abilities and limitations, I plan to participate in workshops and seminars focusing on communication skills and difficult conversations with patients. Additionally, seeking feedback from my peers, supervisors, and patients will ensure continued growth and refinement of my interpersonal skills.

In conclusion, professionalism is a multifaceted concept that entails a delicate balance of technical expertise, emotional intelligence, and effective communication. Difficult conversations with patients challenge our abilities and limitations, prompting self-regulated learning and reflection upon our practice. Through self-awareness, feedback, and continuing education we can develop the skills required to manage these conversations in an empathic and effective manner, ultimately benefitting our patients and their families.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: please rewrite this reflection but focus on professionalism as it pertains to working with colleagues. In the first scenario, talk about my experience watcing a general medicine consultant call a VIDS registrar to yell at them about not taking a patient. The second scenario is about surgical teams being rude when answering calls for consults

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
During my time in MD2, a topic that has significantly challenged my understanding of professionalism is navigating collegial relationships and interactions, specifically when conflicts arise between colleagues. As a future physician, embracing professionalism involves not only exhibiting technical proficiency but also demonstrating collaboration, effective communication, and respect when working with peers. Reflecting on my clinical experiences, two instances stand out that have forced me to reevaluate my approach in engaging with colleagues in a professional manner.

In the first scenario, I was accompanying a general medicine consultant as they reached out to a VIDS registrar to discuss transferring a patient. The conversation quickly escalated, with the general medicine consultant raising their voice and expressing anger towards the registrar for their perceived refusal to accept the patient. The registrar, understandably taken aback, attempted to deescalate the situation, but the consultant remained irate. As a spectator to this interaction, I was troubled by the consultant's behavior, which seemed to contradict the essence of professionalism. This experience highlighted the importance of maintaining composure and remaining respectful in our communications with colleagues, even when facing disagreement or frustration.

In the second scenario, while shadowing a surgical team, I witnessed multiple instances in which team members were terse or dismissive when answering calls for consults. Their attitude seemingly stemmed from the pressure and time constraints under which they operated daily. However, the rudeness exhibited towards their peers hindered effective communication and likely affected the overall wellbeing and collaborative atmosphere among colleagues. This experience solidified my belief that maintaining a polite and respectful demeanor, even in high-pressure environments, is crucial to fostering successful teamwork, ultimately benefitting the care provided to our patients.

These two experiences have been formative in shaping my understanding of professionalism in the context of working with colleagues. Recognizing the potential impact on patient care and team dynamics that these negative professional interactions can have, I am motivated to improve my own abilities and limitations. One of the ways I plan to achieve this is by attending workshops and training sessions that focus on conflict resolution and communication skills specific to the healthcare setting. Additionally, seeking feedback from my peers, supervisors, and mentors will ensure continued growth and refinement of my ability to navigate challenging workplace interactions.

In conclusion, professionalism is a multifaceted concept that extends beyond technical expertise to encompass collaboration, effective communication, and respect when working with colleagues. Conflicts between peers challenge our abilities and limitations and necessitate self-regulated learning and reflection. By fostering self-awareness, receiving feedback and pursuing continuing education, we can cultivate the skills necessary to handle difficult conversations and interactions with colleagues in a respectful and professional manner. This not only ensures a healthier work environment but also contributes to the overarching goal of providing high-quality care to our patients.
